title: Binary Search
description: Given a sorted array of integers nums and a target value, return the index of the target if found. If not, return -1.
hints: The array is sorted. How can you eliminate half the search space each time?
Compare the middle element with the target. Go left or right accordingly.
Set low=0, high=n-1. While low <= high, compute mid. If nums[mid] == target, return mid. If less, low=mid+1. If more, high=mid-1.
follow_ups: What if you need to find the first or last occurrence?
What about searching in a rotated sorted array?

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description: An integer array sorted in ascending order is rotated at some pivot. Given the rotated array and a target, return the index of the target or -1. All values are unique.
hints: You can still use binary search, but you need to figure out which half is sorted.
At any mid point, one half of the array is always sorted. Check if target falls in the sorted half.
If left half is sorted (nums[low] <= nums[mid]): check if target is in [low, mid]. If right half is sorted: check if target is in [mid, high].
follow_ups: Can you find the rotation point?

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
description: A sorted array of unique integers is rotated between 1 and n times. Find the minimum element. The original array was in ascending order.
hints: The minimum is at the rotation point. How does binary search help find it?
Compare mid with the rightmost element. If mid > right, minimum is in the right half.
If nums[mid] > nums[high], minimum is in [mid+1, high]. Else it's in [low, mid]. Converge to the answer.
follow_ups: Can you find the maximum in O(log n)?

title: Koko Eating Bananas
description: Koko can eat bananas at speed k (bananas per hour). She has piles of bananas and h hours. Find the minimum k such that she can eat all bananas within h hours. Each hour she picks one pile and eats k bananas from it.
hints: You're searching for a value k in a range. The answer has a monotonic property — if k works, k+1 also works.
Binary search on the answer! Search for k in range [1, max(piles)].
For each candidate k, compute total hours needed: sum of ceil(pile/k) for each pile. If total <= h, try smaller k.
follow_ups: What other problems can be solved with binary search on the answer?
What if she must eat piles in order?
